planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; silver dollar gum is fast-growing and may require pruning to maintain as a shrub rather than a large tree. In zone 9 it can reach 25–40 feet but is most commonly grown and coppiced as a large shrub for foliage production. The round, silver-blue juvenile leaves (the 'silver dollars') are the primary ornamental feature and are highly valued as cut foliage; adult leaves are narrower and less ornamental.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; silver dollar gum is one of the most widely grown and commercially important ornamental eucalyptus species in warm-climate horticulture — prized almost exclusively for the extraordinary beauty of its juvenile foliage. The juvenile leaves of E. cinerea are perfectly circular to nearly circular, stalkless (clasping the stem), and a beautiful, soft, silver-blue to gray-blue color, arranged in opposite pairs that create a precise, architectural pattern of round blue-silver coins along the young stems — the 'silver dollars' of the common name. These round, blue-silver juvenile leaves are the standard cut-foliage 'gum leaf' used worldwide in floral arrangements, wedding floristry, and decorative foliage, exported in massive quantities from Australian and California eucalyptus plantations. The foliage is aromatic — the eucalyptus oil in the leaves releasing the characteristic refreshing, camphor-eucalyptus scent when crushed. To maintain juvenile foliage (which is the entire ornamental point), the tree can be coppiced (cut to the ground) every few years, stimulating the continuous production of young, round-leaved growth. As a zone 9 garden tree it provides outstanding silver foliage and drought tolerance; as a coppiced cut-foliage plant it provides continuous stem material for floristry.

subcategory: weeping ornamental gum with pink-red flowers

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; silver princess is native to the rocky granite outcrops of southwestern Western Australia — it requires excellent drainage and lean, low-phosphorus soil. It blooms in late winter and spring (July–November in Australia). The cultivar 'Silver Princess' (subsp. magna) has larger, deeper-pink to bright-red flowers and is the most ornamental form. The silvery-white powdery bark (the 'caesia' of the species name, meaning blue-gray) is an additional ornamental feature.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; silver princess is regarded by many Australian plant enthusiasts as the single most ornamental of all the mallees (multi-stemmed, shrubby eucalyptus) — combining several outstanding ornamental qualities in one plant. The form is broadly weeping to pendulous, with graceful, arching branches sweeping to the ground. The bark is silvery-white and powdery (caesia = blue-gray), giving the stems a ghostly, glaucous-white texture. The buds are spectacular: large, urn-shaped, covered in white, waxy powder with long, conical, pointed caps — some of the most beautiful eucalyptus buds of any species. The flowers, when the caps fall, are deep pink to crimson-red to vivid rose-pink with contrasting golden-yellow stamens — large (3-inch), showy, spider-like clusters of brightly colored stamens against the silver-white branch structure. The combination of the weeping form, powdery-silver bark, spectacular urn-shaped buds, and vivid pink-red flowers makes it a multi-season ornamental of exceptional beauty. Native to a small area of granite outcrops in southwestern Western Australia; in cultivation it requires good drainage and thrives in zone 9 warm, dry conditions.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; coral gum is from the dry, hot interior goldfields region of Western Australia — requiring hot, dry conditions and excellent drainage. It blooms over a very long period (spring through summer, often year-round in warm climates) producing the distinctive coral-red to pink, ribbed, lantern-shaped buds and flowers. More reliable in hot, dry, inland Mediterranean climates than in humid coastal conditions.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; coral gum is one of the most colorful and most reliably floriferous of all ornamental eucalyptus — a small to medium-sized mallee or tree from the hot, arid goldfields of the Western Australian interior that produces over a very long season (often effectively year-round in warm climates) a succession of distinctive, beautiful coral-red to bright-pink flowers. The flower buds are themselves highly ornamental: angular, ribbed, lantern-shaped, coral-red to pink caps of distinctive sculptural form that remain on the tree in developing clusters for weeks before the cap falls and the dense, pink-red stamen clusters expand. The flowers, when open, are large clusters of vivid coral-red to rosy-pink, crimson-tipped stamens against the gray-green foliage — a rich, warm-pink to coral-red that is among the most vivid flower colors produced by any Australian tree. The combination of long bloom season, ornamental bud form, vivid flower color, and compact size (15–25 feet) makes it one of the best ornamental eucalyptus for the zone 9 garden. Native to the hot, dry goldfields of WA — a landscape of red soils, sparse desert vegetation, and blazing summer heat — it is thoroughly adapted to the hot, dry conditions of zone 9 summers.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in well-drained to moist, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; white ironbark is more moisture-tolerant than most ornamental eucalyptus — native to the woodland and mallee scrub of southeastern South Australia and western Victoria where annual rainfall is higher. It blooms in winter through spring, with flower color varying from white to cream to pink to vivid red (subsp. rosea, the most ornamental pink form). One of the most cold-tolerant ornamental flowering eucalyptus, surviving moderate frosts.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moist, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; white ironbark is one of the most adaptable and widely planted ornamental flowering eucalyptus — valued for its very long winter-to-spring blooming period, its moderate cold hardiness compared to tropical Australian species, and the variable, attractive flower color that ranges from white through cream, pink, and crimson depending on form. The subspecies rosea produces particularly vivid rose-pink to crimson-red flowers and is the most commonly grown ornamental form. The flowers are typical eucalyptus form — dense, brush-like clusters of stamens expanding from the falling operculum cap — in white, cream, or pink/red, providing nectar for birds throughout the winter and spring. The bark is smooth and white to pale gray-cream with shed patches of darker bark creating a mottled or streaked appearance. It is native to the drier woodlands of southeastern South Australia and western Victoria — a Mediterranean-climate woodland eucalyptus that tolerates more winter rainfall and moderate frost than the arid interior species, making it among the most adaptable ornamental gums for zone 9 coastal gardens. Outstanding as a street tree and garden specimen where both form and winter flowers are valued.

subcategory: dark-barked ornamental gum with pink to red flowers

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; red ironbark is one of the more cold-hardy ornamental eucalyptus — surviving to zone 7b in sheltered, well-drained positions. It is a long-lived tree with deeply furrowed, dark-red to almost black, persistent ironbark that provides year-round structural interest. The flower color is variable — white, cream, pink, and deep crimson forms occur, with 'Rosea' being the most ornamental dark-pink selection. Excellent for birds.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; red ironbark is the most structurally dramatic ornamental eucalyptus for cold climates (surviving to zone 7b) — a long-lived, stately tree developing over decades a deeply furrowed, persistent, very dark red-brown to almost black-red ironbark (the iron-like, very hard, deeply grooved bark that never sheds as in the smooth gums) that creates a powerful, dark, architectural trunk structure of great character in mature specimens. The contrast of the very dark, almost black ironbark with the blue-gray-green, lance-shaped leaves and, in winter and spring, clusters of white to cream to pink to vivid crimson-rose flowers (depending on form) is a multi-season, multi-textural ornamental composition of considerable sophistication. The dark bark catching winter light against pink flowers in the background blue-gray of the canopy is one of the most striking winter tree compositions available in zones 7–11. The cultivar 'Rosea' produces the most vivid, deep-pink to rose-red flowers. Outstanding as a long-term specimen tree where its slow development into a structurally impressive large tree is a generational investment in a garden.
